original_abstract:
As Europe undergoes a significant demographic transition, ensuring the social and economic integration of its aging population has become a primary policy priority. This volume investigates the multifaceted nature of social exclusion and deprivation among Europeans aged 50 and older, utilizing data from the fifth wave of the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE). By introducing innovative multidimensional indices, the contributing authors explore how material and social hardships intersect with physical and mental health, labor market participation, and intergenerational support systems across 15 countries.

The analysis reveals a distinct geographic gradient, with higher levels of material and social deprivation concentrated in Southern and Eastern Europe compared to the more inclusive Nordic and Continental welfare regimes. Key findings emphasize that while material deprivation tends to decrease with age, social isolation and service inaccessibility often intensify among the oldest old. Furthermore, the volume explores critical links between exclusion and health outcomes, demonstrating how sensory impairments, cognitive decline, and restricted access to healthcare exacerbate vulnerability. In the economic sphere, the research highlights how job quality, digital literacy, and pension reforms influence the timing of retirement and the risk of late-life poverty. By examining the role of informal family care and formal long-term care insurance, the authors identify significant gaps in current social protection systems. Collectively, these studies provide empirical evidence to support the development of targeted, inclusive policies aimed at fostering active aging and safeguarding the well-being of Europe’s older citizens in an increasingly complex social landscape.
humanized_abstract:
demographic change has become one of the major policy challenges in Europe. Integrating the elderly socially and economically is considered very important in this context. This volume analyses in detail the complex structures and patterns of social exclusion and deprivation among European older adults (50+). It is based on data from the 5th wave of the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) for 13 EU member states, Cyprus, Latvia and Poland. The book presents for the first time a multidimensional approach by calculating new composite indices of material and social deprivation which allow the detailed examination of how different types of hardship may affect each other and interact differently with physical and mental health, work situation and family networks. As such, it gives for the first time a comprehensive overview of old age poverty in 15 European countries.

The volume makes available a collection of studies probing the interplay of various mechanisms and arenas through which social exclusion affects vulnerable members of European societies across the life course. Based on the common EURAG European survey method, using innovative analyses, such as multiple correspondence analysis, it sheds light on their composition and mapping the material and social composition of exclusions along different welfare regime patterns and country specific conditions in a dynamic and changing society; delves deeper into age-related trends and inequalities by scrutinizing deprivation dimensions occurring along the life-span through age-specific exploration in contrast to an overall European picture; addresses possible channels or factors affecting the onset or deepening of exclusion of the oldest old; searches for the mutual links and impacts between exclusion and poor health, dealing in depth with aspects such as disability, age-related illnesses, hearing and sight losses affecting older people's capacities, access to and quality of healthcare and explores other fields where exclusion may negatively influence the well-being of all ages and in particular the old such as labor conditions, computer literacy, implications of pension policies and how they influence the decision to enter or stay in the labor market at advanced age. Finally, the volume examines how informal family-based care and statutory long-term care insurance schemes and how these interact cover adequately the needs of the older European population, based on empirical findings that can inspire social policy makers when drawing up more responsive and more efficient policies aimed at securing that older Europeans can ages confidently and in decent social and material conditions.
